verses.commentary: Progressive revelation through history culminates in Christ. The prophets spoke in fragments; Jesus is the complete Word. God's speech pattern moves from partial to final, shadow to substance.
The Son is heir of all things and agent of creation. He stands at both ends of history: Creator and inheritor. All things exist through Him and for Him. The 'last days' began with His coming.

Three truths define the Son: radiance of God's glory (eternal emanation), exact representation of His being (perfect imprint), and sustainer of all things by His powerful word. After purification for sins, He sat down—work complete, position secured.
Inheritance secures superiority. The name 'Son' places Him above angels. Judaism elevated angels; Hebrews demolishes angel-worship by establishing Christ's supremacy from the outset.
Psalm 2:7 and 2 Samuel 7:14 applied to Christ. No angel receives sonship language. The rhetorical question demands the answer: none. Sonship is exclusive to Christ.
The firstborn (prototokos) denotes preeminence and supremacy, not creation. Angels worship the Son. They are servants; He is sovereign. Worship distinguishes deity from creature.
Angels are transient servants—spirits and flames. They change form, carry out tasks, and pass away. Their nature is ministerial, not eternal.
The Father addresses the Son as God. Psalm 45:6 applied to Christ proves His deity. The throne is eternal, the scepter righteous. The Son reigns as God forever.
Moral perfection precedes exaltation. Christ loved righteousness and hated wickedness. Therefore God anointed Him with joy above all companions. Holiness qualifies for enthronement.
Psalm 102:25, addressed to Yahweh in the Old Testament, is here applied to Christ. He laid the foundations of the earth and made the heavens. Clearly, Christ is identified as Creator-God.
Creation perishes; Christ remains. The heavens and earth wear out like a garment, subject to decay. But the Son is immutable. He stands outside time and decay.
He will roll up creation like a robe and change it. Yet He remains unchanged. His years never end. Immutability belongs to God alone, here ascribed to Christ.

Psalm 110:1 is the most quoted Old Testament text in the New Testament. No angel receives this invitation to enthronement. Sitting at God's right hand signifies completed work, shared authority, and ultimate victory over enemies.
Angels are ministering spirits sent to serve believers. Their purpose is functional and temporary: to assist those inheriting salvation. Contrast with the Son's eternal kingship is absolute.
